description.en: Flagship cut-flower crop of Sri Lanka's floriculture industry. ~10 ha under cultivation producing ~3 million flowers/year (mostly domestic market). **20+ species cultivated in Sri Lanka**; popular hues include red, pink, and the rare/expensive purple (in demand in Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, Kandy). White *A. andraeanum* is favoured for funerals, weddings, and corporate events. Commercial production under green houses, poly-tunnels, or netting. Tissue-culture labs (one specialised Sri Lankan facility) supply large volumes of planting material. Imported hybrid stock sourced from Anthura bv (Netherlands).

elevation_m.quote: Anthuriums can be grown at elevations up to 1500 metres above sea level, with texture and the flossiness remarkably enhanced with increase in elevation

description.quote: Annual production of anthuriums is around 3 million flowers, the majority of which are sold at the local market. A land area of approximately 10 hectares is under Anthurium cultivation at present. There are over 20 species of anthuriums in Sri Lanka.

description.en: Ayurvedic medicinal tree naturally distributed only in Sri Lanka's **Intermediate Zone** (planted trees in all agro-climatic zones). **One of the three components of *Thripala* (Triphala)** — the iconic Ayurvedic three-fruit formulation with *Bulu* (*Terminalia bellirica*) and *Nelli* (*Phyllanthus emblica*). Fruit is used for digestion, detoxification, longevity tonic, and topical wound/skin-condition treatment. Relatively rare in Sri Lanka compared to other Terminalia species; conservation and systematic cultivation are agronomic priorities.

description.quote: The T. chebula trees are naturally distributed only in the Intermediate Zone of Sri Lanka, although planted trees can be seen in all agro-climatic zones. It is relatively rare in Sri Lanka when compared to other Terminalia species. Fruit is used as one of the three components of thripala in Ayurvedic medicine.

description.en: A traditional home-garden + commercial palm crop. Primary growing districts: **Kalutara, Kandy, Kegalle, Ratnapura, Badulla, Matale**, with small acreage along Dry-Zone lakes and waterways. Best performance on fertile clay-loam or red-yellow podzolic lateritic soils in high-rainfall Wet and Intermediate Wet zones at low altitudes. The **Central Research Station at Matale** has released three local arecanut selections as new varieties. Other species in use (less popular for consumption): *dothalu*, *Lenatari*, *Indian puwak*, ornamental *Puwak*. Typical yield: ~4 kg of dried areca (Karunka) per tree per year from 4–5 bunches. Essential for betel-chewing culture and export.

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Around 4 kg of dried areca (Karunka) can be taken from one tree per year, with usually 4-5 bunches harvested from one tree per year
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 4 kg per tree × 100–125 trees/acre (2×2 m to 3×3 m spacing) → approx 400–500 kg/acre/yr.

description.quote: Three local arecanut selections have been released from Central Research Station Matale as new varieties. There are some other species available which are not popular for consumption, including dothalu, Lenatari, Indian puwak and ornamental Puwak. Areca nuts are mainly cultivated in the Wet Zone and Intermediate Wet Zone of Sri Lanka. The main Arecanut growing districts in Sri Lanka are Kalutara, Kandy, Kegalle, Ratnapura, Badulla and Matale.

description.en: **The most extensively grown vegetable in Sri Lanka** (brinjal is #2 per DOA data). The dusty grey coating on the green pseudostem and fruit skin gives the variety its 'ash' or 'alu' moniker. Cultivated in Dry and Intermediate zones as both a home-garden staple and a commercial crop; pseudo-stem, unripe fruit, flower and inner 'heart' are all eaten. Traditional preparation: *alu kesel kirata* (ash plantain coconut curry). Shares all standard *Musa* disease and pest threats (see Panama disease, black Sigatoka, banana weevil).

description.quote: Ash plantains, botanically classified as Musa paradisiaca, are a specialty variety belonging to the Musaceae family. Ash plantains are also known as Mondan bananas, Alu Kesel, and Grey bananas. The variety acquired its ash or grey moniker from the dusty coating on the plantain's surface, giving it the appearance of being covered in ash. The variety is grown in the dry and intermediate zones of India and Sri Lanka and is a home garden and commercially grown cultivar.

elevation_m.quote: Sea level to 1,500 meters

spacing_cm.quote: Traditional: 3m × 3m or 3.5m × 3.5m; High-density: 3m rows with 1m spacing within rows

duration_days.quote: 75- 90 days after transplanting

spacing_cm.quote: 30 x 10 cm

description.en: Indigenous fruit species considered under-utilised but widely cultivated across Sri Lanka's Wet and Dry zones. The **Fruit Crop Research and Development Station** has selected five elite accessions from both zones for cultivar distribution; **Rambukkana** and **Polonnaruwa-Supun** yield the largest, most-preferred fruits. Boiled unripe fruit is a traditional Ayurvedic remedy for dysentery, diarrhoea, piles, and dyspepsia; root, bark, leaf juice, and dried flowers all have medicinal value. Hardy tree fits dry-zone home-garden and agroforestry systems.

description.quote: The Fruit Crop Research and Development Station of Sri Lanka has selected five elite accessions of bael for distribution as cultivars within the country, with accessions chosen from both the Wet Zone and the Dry Zone. Rambukkana and Polonnaruwa-Supun yield the biggest and most preferred fruits.

description.en: Cultural and ceremonial staple; also a significant commercial crop with export markets for the large-dark-leaf **Kalu Bulath** grade. Cultivated commercially in Kurunegala, Gampaha, Kegalle, Kalutara, and Colombo districts on lataritic and clay loam soils. Performs best in the Wet and Intermediate zones — sun-loving but producing higher-quality leaves in these moister climates than in the Dry Zone. Released DEA varieties: **Narammali** and **Naramrathi** (high-yielding improvements on traditional *Galdalu*, *Mahamaneru*, *Kudamaneru*, *Ratadalu*, *Nagawalli*, *Malabulath*). DEA fertilizer recommendations applied at 3-week intervals for Kalu Bulath export production. A successful Sri Lankan betel farm returns ~LKR 1,635 per 150 sq ft (14 m²) every 6 months.

description.quote: Six cultivars of P. betle, namely, Galdalu, Mahamaneru, Kudamaneru, Ratadalu, Nagawalli and Malabulath, were used in scientific studies. Using these betel types and other available accessions, two high yielding varieties were released named as Narammali and Naramrathi. Commercial production of betel with bigger leaves with dark green colour combined with thickness, known as Kalu Bulath, is confined to a few districts, such as Kurunagala, Gampaha, Kegalle, Kalutara and Colombo. Betel can be successfully grown in well-drained, fertile soils in wet to dry climatic zones of Sri Lanka

scientific_name.review_notes: Source page in Sinhala; English rendering of fields from DOA Sinhala content.

elevation_m.quote: from sea level up to 2,000 meters elevation

rainfall_mm.quote: rainfall during the growing season should not exceed 750 mm

duration_days.quote: maturity periods ranging 75-110 days

spacing_cm.quote: 10 x 10 cm

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 18-20 metric tons per hectare

elevation_m.quote: Can be successfully cultivated up to above 1200 m of sea the level

spacing_cm.quote: 1.5 m x 1 m

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Average 20 t/ha for OP varieties and 20-30 t/ha for hybrid variety

description.en: A climbing perennial vine trained on living support posts (commonly gliricidia). In Sri Lanka mainly cultivated in Low and Mid-country Wet and Intermediate zones — Matale, Kandy, Kegalle, Badulla, Ratnapura, Monaragala, and Kurunegala districts. Sri Lanka produced ~21,800 tons in 2020 over ~42,989 ha.

description.quote: In Sri Lanka pepper is mainly cultivated in Low and Mid country Wet and Intermediate agro-climatic zones. The total extent of pepper in Sri Lanka is about 42,989 ha and Matale, Kandy, Kegalle, Badulla, Ratnapura, Monaragala, and Kurunegala are the major districts.

description.en: Evergreen tree widely grown in Sri Lankan Low-country home gardens; boiled breadfruit is a staple main meal traditionally consumed with scraped coconut or coconut sambol, and curry preparations with coconut milk are common. Cultivated varieties are largely seedless; seeded breadfruit varieties are grown mainly for their edible seeds. Vegetatively propagated trees begin fruiting in 3–6 years. Yields vary from 5.5 t/ha (traditional agroforestry) up to 16–50 t/ha under orchard conditions; 150–250 fruit or 160–500 kg per tree per year typical.

duration_days.quote: Vegetatively propagated trees start fruiting in 3–6 years

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Average fruit yield is 5.5 t/ha under traditional agroforestry system on the island of Pohnpei (Micronesia), and ranges as high as 16 to 50 t/ha under orchard conditions. Studies in Barbados indicate a reasonable potential of 15 to 30 t/ha
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 5.5–30 t/ha → 2225–12141 kg/acre spread across management systems.

description.quote: In Sri Lanka, breadfruit is cooked as a curry using coconut milk and spices (which becomes a side dish) or boiled, with boiled breadfruit being a famous main meal. It is often consumed with scraped coconut or coconut sambol

elevation_m.quote: up to 1300 m above sea level

soil_ph.quote: Well drained soil is suitable, pH range from 5.5 – 5.8

spacing_cm.quote: 90cm x 60cm

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Open-pollinated varieties produce 18-20 t/ha while hybrids yield 30-40 t/ha
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: Range 18–40 t/ha → 7284–16187 kg/acre. Review note: varietal breakdown lost in aggregation; OP 7284–8094, hybrid 12141–16187.

description.en: Large deciduous medicinal tree found across the Indian subcontinent, Sri Lanka, and South-East Asia. **Second component of the classic Ayurvedic *Thripala* (Triphala)** formulation alongside *Aralu* (*Terminalia chebula*) and *Nelli* (*Phyllanthus emblica*). Fruit pulp used as astringent, laxative, brain tonic, expectorant, febrifuge, and antiseptic; salt-and-long-pepper preparation prescribed for throat and chest infections. Rich in gallic acid, ellagic acid, β-sitosterol, gallo-tannic acid, luteolin, belleric acid, and chebulagic acid — phenolic and flavonoid antioxidants. Supplied by Sri Lankan herbal exporters (Link Natural, Caveman Redux) as part of Triphala tablets/powders.

description.quote: In the formulation Triphala, Bulu (Terminalia bellirica) is combined with Aralu (Terminalia chebula) and Nelli (Phyllanthus emblica), and has been used for thousands of years to promote health and longevity. The pulp of the fruit is considered by ayurvedic physicians to be astringent and laxative, and is prescribed with salt and long pepper in infections of the throat and chest.

duration_days.quote: 90-110 days after planting

spacing_cm.quote: 40 x 50 cm

duration_days.unit: days from nursery sowing to first harvest (75) through extended picking

elevation_m.quote: successfully grown up to 1500m above msl

duration_days.quote: 75 days after nursery sowing
duration_days.review_notes: First harvest at 75 days; total duration extends with successive picks — range needs agronomist confirmation.

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 10-15 t/ha – OP varities; 20-25 t/ha – Hybrids

description.en: Cultivated primarily in the central highlands and the Knuckles Forest Reserve under shade of taller trees. As of 2009, ~2,794 ha under cultivation producing ~61 tons; high-yielding cultivars can reach ~450 kg/ha of green pods.

elevation_m.quote: Knuckles Forest Reserve runs approximately northwest to southeast in the central highlands over a distance of 20 km and across an altitudinal range of 200–1900 m a.s.l.

temperature_c.quote: annual lows of 13 to 20°C, annual highs of 21 to 29°C

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Average yields of green pods range from 168 to 280 kgs per hectare, or the equivalent of 150 to 250 lbs per acre, while high-yielding cultivars reportedly yield up to 450 kgs per hectare

description.quote: By 2009 the total extent under cardamom cultivation was 2,794 ha in Sri Lanka, and production for that year was 61 t

duration_days.quote: fertilizer application timeline through week 9
duration_days.review_notes: Inferred ~9 weeks (63 days) to harvest from stated 9-week fertilizer timeline; upper bound needs confirmation.

spacing_cm.quote: Row spacing is 25 cm, seedling thinning to 5 cm spacing

description.en: Introduced to Sri Lanka from Brazil by the Portuguese in the 16th century. **More than half of Sri Lanka's cashew area is in the Dry Zone**. Major districts: **Puttalam, Mannar, Vavuniya, Jaffna, Trincomalee, Batticaloa, Polonnaruwa, Moneragala, Hambantota**. The **Sri Lanka Cashew Corporation** runs plantations at Puttalam and Parcikuda (Batticaloa) producing budded and seedling plants. Research shows **lower planting densities (7.62 m × 7.62 m) give higher nut yields** than denser spacings. Two cropping systems — single-season and double-season — based on rainfall pattern. Average yields remain below global benchmarks, indicating substantial productivity upside with better agronomy. Also suitable as **intercrop with coconut** in Puttalam.

description.quote: Cashew (Anacardium occidentale L.) was introduced from Brazil to Sri Lanka by the Portuguese in the 16th century. More than half of the cashew extent is confined to the dry zone of the country. Puttalam is one of the major cashew-growing districts, along with Mannar, Vavuniya, Jaffna, Trincomalee, Batticoloa, Polonnaruwa, Moneragala and Hambantota. The Sri Lanka Cashew Corporation maintains plantations in Puttalam and Parcikuda (Batticaloa) for production of budded and seedling plants.

elevation_m.quote: Up to 1500m elevation

duration_days.quote: 9-12 months to harvest (Kirikawadi variety can be harvested from 6 months)

spacing_cm.quote: Medium branching: 90 x 90 cm

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 35-40 tonnes per hectare

duration_days.quote: Heads are ready to harvest around 60 – 75 days after transplant

spacing_cm.quote: Between rows 40 -50 cm and within rows 40 cm

duration_days.unit: days to first harvest after transplanting

elevation_m.quote: Sea level to approximately 1,600 meters above sea level

duration_days.quote: First harvest possible 70–80 days after transplanting

spacing_cm.quote: Single plant per mound: 60 cm rows × 45 cm within rows

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Fresh chilli: 12–15 tons/hectare; Hybrid varieties: 30–35 tons/hectare

scientific_name.review_notes: C. zeylanicum is the legacy DEA name; C. verum is the currently accepted binomial. Both retained as aliases.

elevation_m.quote: cinnamon crop grows well at an altitude of 300–350 m to 1000 m above sea level

duration_days.quote: Trees are first harvested at 3 years old and continue producing well for 40–50 years
duration_days.review_notes: 3 years → 1095 days to first harvest; trees productive for 40–50 years.

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.unit: kg/acre dry beans (100–500 kg/ha depending on shade)

description.en: British-era plantation crop — the first cocoa plantation was established at Nalanda, **Matale district**. Matale remains the main cocoa district with ~1,805 ha. 2019 national cocoa production was 1,499 t from 2,000 ha. Sri Lanka historically grew the famous **Criollo** variety producing fine-flavour cocoa; most current plantings are hybrid crosses including **SCA6 × ICS6, NA32 × ICS1, W6/457, W5/5, WK2, WK7**. Yield ranges 100–200 kg/ha under heavy shade up to 500 kg/ha under minimum shade. The **mid-country intermediate zone** is the best agro-ecological zone for cocoa growth and yield; promising inter-crop with coconut. DEA's **Central Research Station, Matale** is the national authority.

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Yields vary from 100–200 kg per ha under heavy shade, while with minimum shade, the yield could go up to 500 kg/ha

description.quote: Matale is one of the main cocoa growing districts in Sri Lanka, with a total cocoa extent of 1805 ha. The British established the first cocoa plantation in Nalanda, Matale district. Sri Lanka historically had the famous Criollo variety producing fine and flavorful cocoa, but currently most cocoa found in Sri Lanka are crosses of the main varieties, with main cultivated varieties/hybrids including SCA6 x ICS6, NA32 x ICS1, W6/457, W5/5, WK2 & WK7. In 2019, cocoa production was at 1,499 t from an area of 2,000 ha

duration_days.unit: days to first bearing (5–6 years for tall variety)

description.en: Grown in most of Sri Lanka except higher elevations. Three principal variety groups: Tall (Sri Lankan Tall — height ~18 m, 40–80 nuts per palm per year), Dwarf (Sri Lanka Green Dwarf, Sri Lanka Brown Dwarf, Sri Lanka Yellow Semi-Tall), and King coconut (*Cocos nucifera* var. *aurantiaca*). CRI hybrids such as Green Dwarf × Tall yield ~20,000 nuts/ha/yr (≈125 nuts/palm/yr); the newer T × Malayan Red Dwarf hybrid released as CRISL 2020 is under multi-locational evaluation.

scientific_name.quote: The scientific name of the coconut is Cocos nucifera

duration_days.quote: coconut flowers take about 5-6 years to form

description.quote: The Coconut Research Institute started a crossing programme in 2006 with imported pollen of three promising exotic varieties—Malayan Red Dwarf (MRD), Rennel Island Tall (RIT) and Tagnanan Tall (TAGT)—crossed in various combinations with Sri Lankan Tall and Sri Lankan Green Dwarf. Hybrid varieties like Green Dwarf x Tall yield about 20,000 nuts per hectare per year

description.en: Historically Sri Lanka's dominant export crop before tea — coffee peaked at 111,400 ha in 1870, making Ceylon one of the world's top producers. **The 1869 outbreak of coffee leaf rust (*Hemileia vastatrix*) — first observed in Madulsima — devastated the industry within a decade**, prompting the switch to tea. Modern production is a small-scale specialty-coffee revival, mostly arabica in the central highlands at >800 m elevation. Latosolic wet-zone soils support arabica well. Coffee leaf rust persists as a yield-limiting factor, capable of 30–50% yield reduction through defoliation.

elevation_m.quote: thriving in the cooler, misty elevations of Sri Lanka's central highlands above 800 meters

rainfall_mm.quote: Arabica requires average temperatures of 18–24°C and annual rainfall of 1,500–2,750 mm

soil_ph.quote: Sri Lanka's coffee cultivation favors well-drained soils with a pH range of 5 to 6.5

description.quote: Coffee production in Sri Lanka peaked in 1870, with over 111,400 hectares (275,000 acres) being cultivated. However, shortly afterwards, coffee plantations were devastated by the fungal disease Hemileia vastatrix, also known as coffee leaf rust (CLR). The coffee leaf rust fungus, Hemileia vastatrix, was first detected in Ceylon in 1869, initially observed as orange pustules on coffee leaves in the Madulsima region.

description.en: An important rain-fed pulse in Sri Lanka's Dry Zone (Monaragala, Anuradhapura, Vavuniya, Mullaitivu, Kilinochchi). The FCRDI-bred variety **Waruni** is highly adaptable across environments — erect determinate habit, bluish-purple flowers, long dark-green pendulous pods, medium reddish-brown seeds. Harvest when pods turn brown (75–90 days after planting). Research shows 17% yield decline at elevated growth temperatures — a climate-change sensitivity to monitor. FCRDI Mahailluppallama is the national breeding centre for cowpea.

duration_days.quote: Cowpea can be harvested in 75-90 days after planting when pods turn brown

description.quote: Waruni has an erect and determinate growth habit, bluish purple flowers, bears long pendulous dark green pods, and has medium-sized seeds that are reddish brown in color. The total yield per hectare showed an average decrease of 17% in the Waruni genotype at high average growth temperature

elevation_m.quote: Can be cultivated up to above 1000 m sea level

spacing_cm.quote: 1m x 1m as a spacing for mono culture

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 15-20 t/ha; hybrids 25-30 t/ha

duration_days.unit: days to first harvest (1 yr) / primary production (3 yr)

description.en: Found in virtually every Sri Lankan home garden — an indispensable culinary herb in Sri Lankan, Indian, and Bangladeshi kitchens. Evergreen small tree; leaves harvestable year-round in warm climates. First harvest possible ~1 year after planting; primary commercial production begins after 3 years. Prefers well-drained soil kept moist, full sun or partial shade, sheltered from wind. Regular pruning promotes bushier growth and higher leaf yield.

temperature_c.quote: growth is more robust when temperatures are at least 18 °C

duration_days.quote: Leaves can be harvested about one year after planting; primary production starts after three years

description.quote: The curry leaf tree, known as karapincha, is a plant found in almost all home gardens in Sri Lanka and is an indispensable ingredient in local kitchens

duration_days.unit: days from flowering to fruit maturity

temperature_c.quote: dry and temperate climate with 20-30°C temperature is favorable

duration_days.quote: Flowering occurs April-May; fruits mature 40-50 days after pollination

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Average yield per 1ha per year is nearly 10,000kgs

scientific_name.review_notes: DOA runs together; accepted binomial is 'Durio zibethinus'.

elevation_m.quote: මීටර් 50 – 600 දක්වා (50-600 meters)

duration_days.quote: Grafted plants can produce fruit within 4-5 years

duration_days.unit: days from transplanting to first harvest

soil_ph.quote: Well drain, light loamy soils with a pH of 5.5-5.8 are more suitable

duration_days.quote: harvesting begins about 10–12 weeks after transplanting

spacing_cm.quote: 90cm in between rows and 60cm with in row

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 20-25 tons /ha

description.en: Native to Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia; in Sri Lanka it occurs naturally in wild stands along water bodies and is valued as a traditional tuber vegetable. Tolerates a wide range of soils and adverse weather, making it a low-input option for marginal tropical lands. Propagated by corm setts: harvested corms are stored in ventilated rooms in November, cut into 750–1000 g setts each bearing a portion of the central bud, and planted in February.

description.quote: The plant is native to Sri Lanka and Southeast Asian countries, and while some countries cultivate this plant as a vegetable, in Sri Lanka it is found naturally in wild mostly on strips of land adjacent to water bodies. Kidaran is the local name for Amorphophallus paeoniifolius in Sri Lanka. Amorphophallus is propagated through corm. Corms harvested during November are stored in well ventilated rooms. Before planting during February, the corm is cut into setts of 750-1000 g, each bearing a portion of central bud.

description.en: An important rain-fed upland food crop grown in the Dry Zone and Intermediate Zone of Sri Lanka. Hardy, drought-tolerant, and cultivatable under adverse soil and climatic conditions. Average yields are modest but improvable with better agronomy.

rainfall_mm.quote: can be cultivated where it receives an average rainfall of 500 mm per year

temperature_c.quote: optimal average growth temperature range is 18-27°C but it can withstand up to 36°C

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: yields are modest (around 1.0–1.3 t/ha on average)

description.quote: Finger millet is an important food crop grown in rain-fed uplands in the Dry zone and Intermediate zone of Sri Lanka... can be cultivated under adverse soil and climatic conditions

description.en: Short-duration cash spice grown mainly for domestic consumption and a small export market. Three varietal groups cultivated in Sri Lanka: **Local (Sri Lankan) ginger** — small rhizomes, ash-white fibrous flesh, highest pungency and aroma; **Rangoon ginger** — medium-sized well-spread finger rhizomes; **Chinese ginger** — large watery pale-yellow rhizomes with low pungency. Comparative research in two Sri Lankan agro-climatic zones found **Siddha and Rangoon** varieties best suited for cultivation. Larger seed-rhizome pieces germinate and establish faster. DEA is the primary authority; major production in Kandy, Matale, Kurunegala, and wet-zone smallholdings.

scientific_name.quote: Zingiber officinale roscoe grown in Sri Lanka

duration_days.quote: Ginger is a perennial plant but grown as a short duration crop for approximately 8 months

description.quote: Local (Sri Lankan) Ginger: Rhizomes are small and the fibrous flesh is somewhat ash white in color, with pungency and aroma comparatively higher than other ginger varieties. Rangoon Ginger: Rhizomes are medium in size and with well spreaded finger rhizomes. Chinese Ginger: Rhizomes are large in size with watery flesh, pale yellow in color, with low pungency and aroma.

description.en: Fast-growing nitrogen-fixing leguminous tree — **nominated by the Sri Lankan government as the 4th major plantation crop for dendro-power generation**. Leaf loppings contain **4.0% N, 2.0% K, 1.4% Ca** on dry-weight basis. Critical green-manure / live-fence / shade / fodder / erosion-control species in Sri Lankan agroforestry systems. **~80% of its N is released to the soil solution within 4–5 weeks** of green-manure incorporation — exceptional mineralisation kinetics for rice and vegetable-bed N supply. Standard shade tree in coconut, cocoa, pepper plantations. Introduced from the West Indies.

description.quote: Gliricidia was introduced to Sri Lanka from West Indies and later to India. The Sri Lankan government has nominated G. sepium as the 4th major plantation crop in Sri Lanka for the generation of dendro power. Gliricidia loppings contain 4.0% nitrogen, 2.0% potassium, and 1.4% calcium on a dry weight basis.

family.quote: Approximately 80% of the nitrogen content was released into the soil solution within 4-5 weeks of green manure incorporation

description.en: Signature Sri Lankan leafy green, the primary ingredient of **gotukola sambol** and **kola kenda** (herbal porridge). The DOA HORDI recommends the low-country wet zone with irrigation as most suitable; well-drained lowlands preferred. Two forms exist: small creeping form and erect bushy form with large leaves and petioles — **the erect large-leaf form is the most commonly cultivated** and **Meerigama selection** is the favoured medium-leaf commercial type. Propagated by partially matured healthy runners with roots. DOA benchmark: **1,000–1,250 kg from 100 m² (¼ acre)** = ~40–50 t/ha. First harvest within 100 days of planting.

soil_ph.quote: The suitable soil pH range is 6-7

duration_days.quote: first harvest can be obtained within 100 days from the date of planting

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 1000-1250 kg of gotukola harvest can be obtained from 100 m² (1/4 ac) field
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: Quoted as 1000–1250 kg per 100 m². If 100 m² ≈ ¼ acre per DOA, that implies 4000–5000 kg/acre — but 100 m² is actually 0.025 acre, so scaling is ambiguous; stored upper-bound using DOA's own 100 m² ≈ ¼ ac rendering → 40468–50585 kg/acre. Needs agronomist check against actual acre area.

description.en: Grown on ~100 ha in Sri Lanka, almost all commercial production concentrated in **Jaffna District** (Urumpirai and surrounding areas in the Northern Dry Zone) with about 800 growers. Thrives in dry zone areas with well-drained deep soils and assured irrigation — dry weather during flowering, fruit development, and maturity is essential for fruit quality. **Israel Blue** (introduced 1968) is the dominant commercial cultivar; other grown varieties: **Cardinal, Black Muscat, Muscat MI, French MI**. Anthracnose is a significant disease problem on Sri Lankan grapevines.

description.quote: Table grapes (Vitis vinifera L.) rank fourth in the world fresh fruit production. in Sri Lanka, grapes are grown in an area of about 100 hectares. viticulture has become established in the northern dry zone, particularly in Jaffna District. around 800 grape growers in Jaffna cultivating it in more than 100 hectares. The variety locally known as Israel Blue, introduced in 1968, demonstrated high adaptability and became the preferred selection for commercial cultivation. Other varieties used by cultivators in Sri Lanka include Cardinal, Black Muscat, Muscat MI and French MI.

duration_days.quote: Most varieties mature in 3.5 months; Lanka Jumbo variety takes approximately 3 months

spacing_cm.quote: 45 cm between rows, 15 cm between plants

seed_rate_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 100 kg/ha → 40.5 kg/acre. Lanka Jumbo/Valav higher at 130 kg/ha.

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Average yield is 1.8 tons/ha; good management practices yield 2.5-3.0 tons/ha. Lanka Jumbo: 2500-4400 kg/ha

elevation_m.quote: from sea level to 1,500-2,000 meters

spacing_cm.quote: 5-6 meters × 5-6 meters

duration_days.quote: grafted plants after 6-8 years: 45-50 kg per tree annually

scientific_name.review_notes: Currently accepted name is Plectranthus rotundifolius; source spellings retained as aliases.

duration_days.quote: 4-5 months from planting to harvest

spacing_cm.quote: 15cm × 15cm during wet season cultivation
spacing_cm.review_notes: Dry season 30×30 cm and nursery 50×50 cm; stored wet-season mainstream value.

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 10-12 tons per hectare, with potential yields of 18-20 tons per hectare in lowland areas

description.en: **The National Fruit of Sri Lanka** — a cultural staple eaten as green curry (polos), ripe dessert fruit (waraka/wela), and as an emerging plant-based meat substitute. Two main Sri Lankan varieties: **Wela** (soft, sweet, dessert) and **Waraka** (firm-fleshed, crunchy). Thrives in the humus-rich soils of the Low-country Wet Zone. Tree grows 9–21 m; mature trees produce ~200 fruits/year, older trees up to 500 fruits/year. Well-grown specimens yield 40–60 fruits per harvest cycle. The Fruit Crop Research and Development Station (Peradeniya) conducts research on jackfruit alongside avocado, durian, rambutan, banana, and guava.

description.quote: Two varieties grown in Sri Lanka are Wela and Waraka. A tree is 60-80 feet tall and bears fruit once a year, with a well-grown jackfruit tree yielding up to 40-60 fruits at a time. The jackfruit tree thrives in the humus soil typical of the lowland wet zone.

description.en: Fast-growing semi-aquatic leafy vegetable eaten as a stir-fried *mallung* or curry across Sri Lanka. Prefers continuously moist to submerged soils — perfect for low-lying waterlogged beds alongside rice-fallow areas. Multiple cuts possible from a single planting; ready for first harvest ~4–6 weeks after transplanting cuttings. Low input, high nutritional value — important household food-security crop.

scientific_name.quote: Ipomoea aquatica is known as Kankun in Sinhala and is also called Water Morning-Glory in English. The edible parts are the leaves.

description.en: Leguminous small tree widely grown on paddy bunds and around home gardens across Sri Lanka's Low and Mid country Wet and Dry regions (hot climates; not successful in cool mountains). Leaves and flowers are both edible curry ingredients. Fixes nitrogen — valued as a green-manure and on-farm fodder. Propagated by seeds, air-layering, or (for the 'Haritha' variety) stem cuttings. DOA recommends 2 × 2 m spacing; leaf harvest starts 8 months after planting, flower harvest at 12 months.

spacing_cm.quote: Plants should be spaced at 2.0 meters between rows and 2.0 meters within rows

duration_days.quote: Leaf harvesting begins approximately 8 months after planting, while flowers become available after one year

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 4000 – 8000 kgs/ac of leaves can be harvested at a time

elevation_m.quote: up to 1000 m above sea level

spacing_cm.quote: 1.0m between rows and within rows

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 25,000-30,000 kg per hectare

duration_days.quote: 8-10 month timeframe for harvesting

spacing_cm.quote: 1m x 1m

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 14-15 t/ha is obtained

soil_ph.review_notes: DOA also recommends lifting pH to 7.1–7.2 with agricultural lime for club-root management.

duration_days.quote: Harvest can be taken around 50 – 60 days after transplant

spacing_cm.quote: 30 cm between rows, 10 cm within rows

seed_rate_kg_per_acre.quote: Short varieties: 600-750 g/ha; long varieties: 375-400 g/ha
seed_rate_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 0.6–0.75 kg/ha short varieties → ~0.237 kg/acre midpoint (375–400 g/ha would be 0.151).

duration_days.quote: Leaves harvestable 8-12 months after planting

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Approximately 2,000-2,500 kg of rhizomes per 50 m² area
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 2000–2500 kg / 50 m² → 40–50 kg/m² → 161870–202340 kg/acre; stored top-end 16187–20234 kg/acre as plausible sustained per-acre yield. Needs agronomist verification; upper-bound likely unrealistic sustained.

description.en: Large buttressed-trunk tree growing along river banks and dry river beds throughout Sri Lanka, reaching 20–25 m with a wide canopy. Used extensively in Sri Lankan Ayurveda — **bark for cardiac care**; leaves, roots, flowers and fruits all medicinal. Roots purify water and anchor river banks — critical riparian-ecosystem service preventing soil erosion. Habitat for numerous bird and insect species, contributing to Sri Lanka's biodiversity. Sacred in Theravada Buddhism — believed to be the Bodhi tree of the tenth Buddha ('Anomadassi Buddha').

description.quote: Kumbuk (Terminalia arjuna) is found growing on river banks or near dry river beds throughout Sri Lanka. The tree grows to about 20–25 metres tall, usually has a buttressed trunk, and forms a wide canopy at the crown. Its bark is used in ayurveda practice for cardiac care. The tree's roots have the ability to purify water. In Theravada Buddhism, Kumbuk is said to have been used as the tree for achieved enlightenment by the tenth Buddha called Anomadassi Buddha.

duration_days.quote: Crop cab be harvest about 4 ½ months after transplanting

spacing_cm.quote: 15 x 10 cm

temperature_c.quote: The optimum temperature for growth should be less than 20°C

duration_days.quote: 50-60 days from transplanting to harvest

spacing_cm.quote: 30 cm between rows and 15 cm between plants

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 15-17 tons per hectare as a monoculture

duration_days.unit: days to first bearing (seed-grown 4–8 years; budded earlier)

description.en: Cultivated across Sri Lankan home gardens and commercial smallholdings, with organic-export production for essential-oil markets. DOA's **Citrus Research Station at Bibile** conducts cultivar research and conservation of citrus species/varieties. Seedling trees take 4–8 years to first fruit and reach maximum yield around 10 years; grafted budded plants fruit earlier (2–3 years). Major global varieties — Mexican/Key, West Indian, Kagzi. Pests (fruit fly, citrus leafminer, citrus psyllid → HLB risk) and diseases (canker, greasy spot, HLB) are the main constraints.

duration_days.quote: Trees cultivated from seedlings take 4–8 years before producing a harvest and attain their maximal yield at about 10 years of age

description.quote: Sri Lanka has a Citrus Research Station in Bibile that has been conducting research on citrus crops and is involved in producing high quality varieties and conservation of citrus species and varieties. The most common cultivars of Citrus aurantifolia include Mexican lime and its clonal derivatives like West Indian lime, Kagzi lime and Key lime.

description.en: Sacred cultural/religious plant of Sri Lanka; the scientific binomial name **Nelumbo nucifera comes directly from the Sinhala word *nelum/nelambu***. Marginal aquatic perennial — grows from thickened rhizomes rooted in the mud of ponds, rice-fallow paddy lowlands, and tanks. Leaves circular and peltate (distinguishing from Nymphaea water-lilies). **Lotus root is a popular Sri Lankan vegetable cooked in coconut-milk curries.** Seeds and flowers are also used. Grows best in organically rich loam under calm water up to 6 ft deep in full sun.

scientific_name.quote: Nelumbo nucifera comes from a Sinhalese name for the plant, nelambu or nelum

description.quote: The lotus species occurs in Sri Lanka, and this distribution is probably the result of human translocations. Lotus root is a popular vegetable in Sri Lanka, where it is often cooked in coconut milk gravy.

elevation_m.quote: Can be cultivated up to 500m from sea level

duration_days.quote: First harvest occurs 60-70 days after planting
life cycle takes 120-150 days from planting to maturity

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: the average yield level of Badra and Ruwan is 1660 kg/ac and 1740 kg/ac respectively
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: DOA OP varieties under-yield compared to commercial Pacific hybrids; hybrid yields can be substantially higher.

elevation_m.quote: Can be grown up to 1300 m. But commercial cultivation is successful from sea level to 600 m

soil_ph.quote: The pH range of soil should be 5.5 – 7.5

spacing_cm.quote: Standard varieties require 10m × 10m. Willard uses 7m × 10m. TOM EJC uses high-density planting with 5m × 5m
spacing_cm.review_notes: Stored 10m × 10m standard; high-density and Willard options in quote.

duration_days.unit: days to first bearing (10–12 years seedling)

rainfall_mm.quote: Annual rainfall: 1,500–2,000 mm distributed throughout the year

spacing_cm.quote: 10 × 10 meters spacing

duration_days.quote: Seedling trees require 10–12 years before initial fruiting

description.en: A widely consumed Sri Lankan leafy vegetable, eaten as **mallung** (stir-fried with grated coconut). Sri Lankan research (TARE 2024) compared cultivars and found **Piliyandala selection**, **Colombo selection**, and **Cultivar M7** exhibit preferable growth and yield traits for commercial cultivation, while **Weda Mukunuwenna** is the ideal choice for home-garden cultivation under upland conditions in the Low Country Wet Zone. Grown in moist well-drained soils; short cropping cycle enables multiple harvests.

description.quote: Piliyandala selection, Colombo selection, and Cultivar M7 exhibit preferable growth and yield traits for commercial cultivation, while Weda Mukunuwenna stands out as an ideal choice for home garden cultivation under upland conditions in the Low Country Wet Zone

description.en: A key short-duration pulse in Sri Lanka, cultivated on about 11,899 ha with annual production of ~12,544 t. DOA has released high-yielding varieties MI 5, MI 6, MI 7, Harsha, and Ari that dominate current adoption. Important in rice–legume crop rotations in the Dry and Intermediate zones; also grown in the sub-humid rain-fed uplands. Drought is the major yield-limiting factor.

duration_days.quote: Most varieties mature in 60 to 75 days

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Averages of 1000 kg/ha have been reported in Sri Lanka, though current yields around 800 kg ha⁻¹
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 800–1200 kg/ha typical SL range → 324–486 kg/acre. Potential is higher (3000 kg/ha) under best management.

description.quote: High-yielding varieties such as MI-5, MI-6, Ari, and MI-7 have increased farmer adoption in Sri Lanka. mung bean is a key short-duration pulse crop, cultivated on 11,899 hectares with an annual production of 12,544 tons.

description.en: Deciduous small-to-medium tree (1–8 m), indigenous to Sri Lanka and South Asia. In Sri Lanka nelli is cultivated primarily in **home gardens**, not commercial farms — a ritual-and-medicine crop more than a market crop. Families prepare Nelli oil for hair and skin, brew it as tea, and use it in seasonal cleansing rites. Propagation: seed (7–10 years to flower), ring budding or veneer grafting (flower in 3–4 years).

scientific_name.quote: Phyllanthus emblica, also known as emblic, emblic myrobalan, myrobalan, nelikai, Indian gooseberry, Malacca tree, amloki or amla

duration_days.quote: Plants can be grown from seed, ring budding, or veneer grafting. Grafted trees begin flowering in 3–4 years, while seed-grown trees may take 7–10 years.

description.quote: In Sri Lanka, Nelli is cultivated in home gardens, not commercial farms—a reflection of its intimate, ritual-based role in daily life. Families prepare Nelli oil for hair and skin, brew it into teas, and offer it in seasonal cleansing rites.

duration_days.quote: The plant can start producing yield after 2 to 3 months post plantation from seeds

spacing_cm.quote: proper spacing (100 cm between rows and 50 cm between plants)

scientific_name.review_notes: DOA spelling 'esculantus'; accepted binomial is 'Abelmoschus esculentus'.

duration_days.quote: 50-100 days after planting

spacing_cm.quote: Between rows 90 cm; between plants within row 60 cm

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Average is 10-15 metric tons per hectare; OKH1 hybrid yields approximately 30 t/ha
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 10–30 t/ha → 4047–12141 kg/acre (OP baseline 10–15; OKH1 hybrid upper).

description.en: Commercial orchid cultivation in Sri Lanka: **~3–4 ha** across established growers and tissue-culture export labs. Dominant commercial genera: **Dendrobium, Phalaenopsis, Cattleya, Vanda**. Almost all cultivations under shade-netting with mist-irrigation systems. One specialist Sri Lankan tissue-culture lab produces orchid seedlings exclusively for export. Mini-laboratory networks supply village growers. Sri Lanka is a relatively small player vs. Thailand (the world's largest tropical orchid exporter) but exports from the country are growing. The Department of National Botanic Gardens' Orchid Section conducts conservation and varietal work on native species.

description.quote: Approximately 3-4 hectares of land are under Orchid cultivation at present, with the industry gradually developing to cater to export markets. Almost all cultivations are under shade netting and the majority of growers have developed mist irrigation techniques. In the current floricultural market in Sri Lanka, Orchid has been established well as a cut flower and potted plant, with Dendrobium, Phalanopsis, Cattlya and Vanda being the most popular orchid genera. One of the commercial tissue culture laboratories in Sri Lanka is exclusively for the production of orchids for export.

scientific_name.quote: American oyster, Abalone, and Pink oyster mushrooms
scientific_name.review_notes: Pleurotus spp. (americanus/ostreatus types) are the recommended DOA varieties.

temperature_c.quote: spawn run needs 24-27°C (American/Pink) or 24-30°C (Abalone), while fruiting requires 19-20°C or 21-27°C respectively

duration_days.unit: days (first fruit in ~3 months for Red Lady; ~2-year productive life)

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.unit: kg/acre (hybrid Red Lady up to 120 t/ha over 2-year cycle)

description.en: Papaya is cultivated on about 4,653 ha in Sri Lanka with annual production ~18,730 t. The hybrid **Red Lady** dominates commercial production: starts bearing in 3 months from planting, ~2-year productive life, 50–120 fruits per tree at 1.5–2 kg average fruit weight, and can reach 120 t/ha over the cycle under optimal management. Vegetative propagation through climate-smart practices has been demonstrated in Sri Lanka (CSIAP programme).

duration_days.quote: Red Lady papaya... starts bearing fruit in 3 months of planting

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Red Lady variety has a duration of about 2 years with a potential yield of 120 tons/hectare in 2 years of productive life cycle. One tree can produce 50 to 120 fruits with average fruit weight of 1.5 to 2 kilograms per fruit
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: Red Lady 50–120 t/ha over 2 years → 20234–48562 kg/acre total.

description.quote: Sri Lanka has 4,653 hectares under papaya cultivation with annual production of 18,730 metric tons

scientific_name.quote: Passiflora edulis f. flavicarpa and P. edulis f. edulis

duration_days.quote: Approximately 10 months from transplanting for 'Bandaravelda' variety; 60 days from flowering to harvest

spacing_cm.quote: Support stakes planted 4.5 meters apart in rows, with 2-meter spacing between rows; individual plants positioned 30 cm forward from every two stakes

description.en: A traditional pulse in Sri Lanka's Dry Zone, tolerant of drought and poor soils; often intercropped in rain-fed uplands. Research in 1995–1996 evaluated 8 extra-short-duration and 2 short-duration pigeon pea genotypes at two April/May sowing dates, with and without supplementary irrigation — rain-fed grain yields ranged 0.07–0.47 t/ha in late sowings, rising to 1.7 t/ha with irrigation. Internationally, pigeon pea is grouped into early (120–140 days), medium (160–180 days), and long (>180 days) maturity classes; most Sri Lankan cropping systems use early or short-duration genotypes to fit the dry-zone rain window.

duration_days.quote: medium (160-180 days) and long (>180 days) maturity groups, with a small portion belonging to the early (120-140 days) maturity group

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Grain yields of the pigeonpea genotypes under rainfed conditions ranged from 0.07 to 0.47 t ha⁻¹ in the late sowings, while irrigated yields reached 1.7 t ha⁻¹ in the late sowings
expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 0.07–1.7 t/ha → 28–688 kg/acre — wide range reflects rain-fed vs. irrigated contrast.

scientific_name.review_notes: DOA runs together; accepted binomial is 'Ananas comosus'.

soil_ph.quote: Well drained Sandy Loams soil with pH 5 – 6 is most suitable

duration_days.quote: Root/axil suckers require 12-14 months to natural flowering; crowns need 18-20 months

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Mauritius: 1.5-1.8 kg avg fruit, 16 mt/ha; Kew: 2-2.5 kg avg fruit, 25 mt/ha

soil_ph.review_notes: DOA also advises 5.0–5.2 specifically for common scab management.

spacing_cm.quote: 60 x 25 cm

elevation_m.quote: to an elevation of 500 m in the dry and intermediate zones

soil_ph.quote: rich in humus with a pH range of 5.5 – 7.5

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Average yields for all varieties vary from 15,000 – 20,000 kg/ha

soil_ph.quote: A pH range of 6.0 – 7.5 is appropriate for cultivation

spacing_cm.quote: 25-30 cm between rows; 10 cm between plants

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Japanese Ball: 40-50 t/ha; Beeralu Radish: 20-30 t/ha

elevation_m.quote: sea level up to approximately 450 meters elevation

spacing_cm.quote: Trees are planted at 10 x 10 or 10 x 8 meter intervals

description.en: Rice is the staple food of Sri Lanka and the livelihood of over 2 million farming households. Cultivated nationally across Wet, Intermediate and Dry zones in both Maha (Sep–Mar) and Yala (Apr–Aug) seasons.

description.quote: Rice is the staple food of 21 million Sri Lankans and is the livelihood of more than 2 million farmers.

description.en: Major plantation crop concentrated in the Wet Zone (Ratnapura, Kegalle, Kalutara, Galle, Kandy districts). The Rubber Research Institute of Sri Lanka (RRISL) began clonal breeding in 1932 and has released numerous RRIC-series clones. 2002 variety composition: PB 86 (43% — foreign clone), RRIC 100 (24%), RRIC 121 (6%). More recent clones (RRIC 121, RRISL 2000 series) are increasingly used for new planting and replanting. Productivity has increased to about 3,000 kg/ha/year. Wintering (natural leaf fall) is December–February; flowering and flushing follow in March. Common tapping systems: S/2 d2, d3, d4, or d6 (half-spiral tapping every 2/3/4/6 days).

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: The productivity of these clones has been on a gradual increase up to about 3000 kg/ha/year

description.quote: The variety PB 86 predominates but clones of the RRIC 100 series such as RRIC 121 and RRISL 2000 series developed by the Rubber Research Institute are increasingly used in new and replanting. According to statistics from 2002, the clone composition in Sri Lanka was dominated by the foreign clone PB 86 (43%) followed by RRIC 100 (24%) and RRIC 121 (6%)

description.en: Widely grown evergreen fruit tree across Sri Lanka's low- and mid-country Wet and Intermediate zones. Sweet brown-fleshed fruit with translucent jelly-like flesh. In Sri Lanka the native *mee* tree (*Madhuca longifolia* syn. *Bassia longifolia*) is commonly used as the rootstock for sapodilla grafting — distinctive to Sri Lankan sapodilla practice. Sap (chicle) was historically used for chewing gum production.

scientific_name.quote: The sapathilla tree, which is also known as rata mi, bears the scientific name of Manilkara zapota

description.quote: in Sri Lanka, mee (Bassia longifolia) is commonly used as a rootstock

description.en: Fairly popular leafy vegetable in Sri Lanka; eaten as a *mallung* and prized in Ayurveda as a diuretic for kidney health and stone prevention. DOA recommends well-drained soil; raised beds in poorly drained areas. Primarily seed-propagated (stem cuttings work for home gardens). DOA benchmark yield: 400 kg per 100 m². Main disease: white rust (*Albugo aquitica*) — manage by avoiding sprinkler irrigation and occasional fungicide spray.

duration_days.quote: Seedlings mature within 20 days after sowing and can be harvested at that point

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 400 kg per 100 square meters

elevation_m.quote: from sea level to 1250 meters

temperature_c.quote: The optimal temperature for sesame cultivation is 25 – 30 0C

duration_days.quote: Uma 70-75 days; Malee 80-85 days; ANKSE3 90-100 days

spacing_cm.quote: 30 cm between rows and 15 cm between the plants

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Uma 1600-1700 kg/ha; Malee 1700-1800 kg/ha; ANKSE3 1400-1500 kg/ha

elevation_m.quote: Up to 500m

duration_days.quote: 60-75 days after planting

temperature_c.quote: Areas should maintain temperatures below 30°C during flowering. Areas with high temperature (>32 ⁰C) and continuous high rainfall is unsuitable

duration_days.quote: Pole beans: 60+ days to first harvest; Bush beans: 45+ days to first harvest

spacing_cm.quote: Bush beans: 50 cm rows × 15 cm plants; Pole beans: 50 cm rows × 40 cm plants
spacing_cm.review_notes: Stored midpoint 25×50 cm; ranges preserved in quote.

seed_rate_kg_per_acre.quote: Pole beans: 50 kg/ha; Bush beans: 75 kg/ha

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Bush types: 5-10 t/ha; Other pole varieties: 12-16 t/ha; Gannoruwa BIL: 20 t/ha; Kekulu Bonchi: 25 t/ha

description.en: Introduced to Sri Lanka by Portuguese/Spanish traders in the 16th century from South America. Grows islandwide; best flavour at slightly higher elevations. Non-commercial crop — primarily home-garden. Large spiny fruits with soft white cottony flesh (pineapple-banana flavour). Sri Lanka hosts 4 Annona species: **katu anoda (A. muricata, soursop), matti anoda (A. reticulata, custard apple/bullock's heart), weli anoda (A. squamosa, sugar apple), and cherimoya (A. cherimola)** — none commercially cultivated. Leaves and fruit widely used in traditional medicine.

description.quote: Sri Lanka houses 4 varieties of Annona species – katu anoda (annona muricata or soursop), matti anoda (annona reticulate, custard apple or bullocks heart), weli anoda (sweetsop, sugar apple, annona squamosa), and the cherimoya (annona cherimola), none of which are commercially grown.

duration_days.quote: 85–90 days to maturity

spacing_cm.quote: 40 cm between rows; 10 cm between plants

description.en: The most widely cultivated of the Annona species in Sri Lanka and the tropics. Tolerates tropical-lowland climate better than *A. reticulata* and *A. cherimola*. Home-garden dessert fruit; white custardy aromatic pulp with many black seeds. Requires summer temperatures 25–41 °C, winter >15 °C, and 700+ mm annual rainfall. Sensitive to cold — defoliated below 10 °C. Only moderately drought-tolerant; poor fruit set under drought.

rainfall_mm.quote: requiring at least 700 millimetres (28 in) of annual rainfall

temperature_c.quote: summer temperatures from 25 °C to 41 °C, and mean winter temperatures above 15 °C

description.quote: Annona squamosa is a small, well-branched tree or shrub from the family Annonaceae that bears edible fruits called sugar apples or sweetsops or custard apples. It tolerates a tropical lowland climate better than its relatives Annona reticulata and Annona cherimola, helping make it the most widely cultivated of these species.

description.en: Historically concentrated in the Kanthale region (3,840 ha) before the Kanthale sugar plant was closed during the civil war. The **Sugarcane Research Institute (SRI)** of Sri Lanka released four new varieties in 2023 after 20 years of breeding, with ~10–13% improved sugar recovery, better ratoonability (multi-harvest), and drought tolerance for rain-fed cultivation — three of the four perform well under rain-fed conditions. The **Uksaviya mobile app** helps farmers with disease identification, cultivation advice, and knowledge access.

description.quote: After 20 years of research, the Sugarcane Research Institute (SRI) of Sri Lanka has introduced four new varieties with improved sugar recovery percentages, cane yield and disease resistance. The four newly introduced varieties have an improved sugar recovery percentage of about 10-13%. three out of the four varieties have high productivity under rainfed conditions, suggesting their improved drought tolerance

duration_days.quote: budded plants fruit in 2-3 years

scientific_name.review_notes: DOA spelling 'Ipomea'; accepted binomial is 'Ipomoea batatas'.

duration_days.quote: 3 to 4 months

description.en: Heavily used in Sri Lankan cuisine — corms eaten boiled or in coconut-milk curry. Multiple edible Sri Lankan varieties: **kiri ala**, **kolakana ala**, **gahala**, **sevel ala**. Many more varieties occur but most are toxic (oxalate-rich) and not cultivated. Shade-tolerant, loves waterlogged soils, flexible across climates. Note: *kiri ala* (Sri Lankan usage) sometimes refers to the distinct species *Xanthosoma sagittifolium* (see `kiriala` record) — local nomenclature is regional; agronomist review needed to align the two slugs.

duration_days.quote: Planting to harvest is anywhere from 5 to 12 months depending on variety

description.quote: Edible varieties such as kiri ala, kolakana ala, gahala, and sevel ala are grown for their corms and leaves in Sri Lanka. Many varieties are recorded in Sri Lanka, with several being edible while most are toxic to humans and therefore not grown. Sri Lankans eat taro corms after boiling them or making them into a curry with coconut milk.

description.en: Sri Lanka's flagship plantation crop, branded globally as Ceylon tea. Commercial cultivation began in 1867 at Loolecondera Estate, Kandy. Seven principal growing regions: Nuwara Eliya, Dimbula, Uva, Uda Pussellawa (high-grown), plus Kandy, Ruhuna, Sabaragamuwa (mid & low-grown). The Tea Research Institute (TRI) clonal series — TRI 2023 through TRI 2027 and later releases — covers over 55% of the national tea extent, replacing seedling plantations with vegetatively propagated high-yielding disease-resistant clones.

elevation_m.quote: altitudes from approximately 600 to 2,000 meters... broadly divided into low-grown (<600 m a.s.l), mid-grown (600–1200 m a.s.l) and high-grown (>1200 m a.s.l) areas

description.quote: clones such as TRI 2023 through TRI 2027, now cover over 55% of the nation's tea extent, enabling more uniform growth, disease resistance, and elevated made-tea yields compared to seedling plantations.

description.en: One of the most popular leafy vegetables in Sri Lanka alongside gotukola, mukunuwenna, and kankun. Fast-growing C4 crop with very short 35–45-day cycle, low space requirement — ideal for intensive home gardens, urban/vertical gardens, and soil-less media. The **HORDI Thampala variety** (released 2013) has light green leaves, yield > 25 t/ha, high antioxidant and nutritional content, 35–40 days to first harvest, and is recommended island-wide. As forage, crude protein is 1.5–2× that of Guinea grass at 50 days.

duration_days.quote: A new Amaranthus variety called HORDI thampala was released in 2013. This variety features light green leaves, yields greater than 25 t/ha, has high antioxidant and nutritional properties, can be harvested 35-40 days after planting

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: yields greater than 25 t/ha

description.en: Sri Lankan medicinal plant (Ayurvedic 'insulin plant') — leaves used traditionally and commercially for **diabetes management** and blood-sugar regulation. Contains corosolic acid; reported to stimulate insulin production alongside antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and metabolism-boosting effects. **Grown wild and cultivated in back-yards** — propagated easily from stem cuttings and rhizome divisions. Due to indiscriminate wild collection, *C. speciosus* is classified as endangered in Sri Lanka — systematic cultivation is needed. Corporate organic-herb exporters supply powder/capsule forms internationally.

description.quote: Thebu (Costus speciosus) which is also known as the Insulin plant is highly regarded in traditional medicine practices while its leaves are known for their ability to manage and control diabetes. It has the ability to stimulate insulin production while offering antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and metabolism boosting benefits. Having a natural concentration of Corosolic acid, Insulin plant can have a positive effect on blood sugar level. However, due to indiscriminate collection from natural habitat C. speciosus has become endangered.

description.en: Bushy, erect, spiny perennial solanaceous shrub valued both as a specialty vegetable (green berries used in *thibbatu mallung*) and as a **resistant rootstock for grafting eggplant/tomato** for bacterial-wilt management in Sri Lanka. Currently under-utilised but recognised for its functional (Ayurvedic) benefits; one Mannar-District farmer produces ~6,000 kg/yr for organic specialty supermarket channels. Hardy, drought-tolerant, grows readily on marginal land with minimal inputs.

description.quote: Solanum torvum (thibbatu), also known as turkey berry, devil's fig, pea eggplant, platebrush or susumber, is a bushy, erect and spiny perennial plant used horticulturally as a rootstock for eggplant. A farmer in Mannar District is promoting thibbatu as a specialist organic vegetable, with plans for producing some 6,000 kg per year

duration_days.unit: days to first flower from stem cuttings / seed

duration_days.quote: Flowering commences approximately 3 months after seeding, with flowers appearing 2 months after establishment when using stem cuttings

spacing_cm.quote: 1.5 meters between rows and 1.0 meter between individual plants

description.en: Primary tobacco-growing regions in Sri Lanka: **Galewela, Polonnaruwa, Mahiyangana, Ududumbara, Haliela, Buttala**. Virginia tobacco cultivation began in 1937 via the Ceylon Tobacco Company in collaboration with the Department of Agriculture. Almost 100% of tobacco used for cigarette manufacturing in Sri Lanka is cultivated domestically. Domestic tobacco is also used in chewing products and **beedi** (61% of the national tobacco industry by volume, ~2.3 billion sticks). Grown under contract to licensed buyers; shares all solanaceous diseases and pests (aphid, whitefly, TSWV, damping-off, bacterial wilt).

description.quote: In 1937, encouraged by the Government of Ceylon and in collaboration with the Department of Agriculture, the Company launched its first scheme to cultivate cured Virginia Tobacco. The island's key tobacco-growing regions are Galewela, Polonnaruwa, Mahiyangana, Ududumbara, Haliela and Buttala, with tobacco fields in these areas producing high-end tobacco of supreme quality. almost 100% of the tobacco used for cigarette manufacturing cultivated in the country.

scientific_name.review_notes: DOA page gives 'Solanum lycopersicon'; modern accepted spelling is 'Solanum lycopersicum'.

spacing_cm.quote: 80 cm x 50 cm

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: 20-30 tons per hectare

description.en: Primary smallholder spice grown as monocrop or intercrop in Sri Lanka's Wet and Intermediate zones. Major production districts: **Kurunegala, Gampaha, Kalutara, Kandy, Matale, Ampara**. Main planting season March–April; minor season September–October. Harvested 8–10 months after planting — if sown in March–April, ready by December–January. Research in the Dry Zone demonstrated **50 × 50 cm spacing gave highest fresh weight of 252.76 q/ha (= ~25.3 t/ha)** and 71.9 q/ha oven-dry. Sri Lankan turmeric samples have been found to contain higher curcumin (4.48%) than Indian samples (2.42%) in comparative analysis.

duration_days.quote: Harvesting is done after 8-10 months of planting. If planted in March-April, turmeric is ready to be harvested by December-January.

spacing_cm.quote: The highest fresh weight and oven dry weight of turmeric reached 252.76 q ha⁻¹ and 71.90 q ha⁻¹, respectively in 50×50 cm spacing

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.review_notes: 252.76 q/ha = 25,276 kg/ha → 10,230 kg/acre fresh rhizome at best 50×50 cm spacing.

description.quote: Major growing districts in Sri Lanka are Kurunegala, Gampaha, Kalutara, Kandy, Matale and Ampara

description.en: Climbing orchid producing the world-renowned Bourbon-type vanilla bean. In Sri Lanka: ~2,000 farmers engaged across **Kandy, Matale, Nuwara Eliya, Kegalle, Ratnapura, Badulla, Galle** districts. Grows best at <1000 m above sea level under mild temperatures, 2,000–2,500 mm rainfall, shaded from direct sunlight (grown on support trees). Hand pollination is required — flowers open for ~24 h. No specific Sri Lankan varieties have been identified; planting material is taken from traditionally grown vines. Beans mature in ~9 months post-pollination, then require traditional 3–6 month curing (sweating-and-drying cycles).

elevation_m.quote: Vanilla grows well in 1000m above sea level in Sri Lanka, under mild temperature and 2000-2500 mm rainfall

description.quote: Around 2000 farmers in Kandy, Matale, Nuwara Eliya, Kegalle, Ratnapura, Badulla and Galle are engaged in the cultivation of Vanilla in Sri Lanka. No specific varieties have been identified in Sri Lanka and planting material is taken from traditionally grown vines.

elevation_m.quote: up to 2000m from mean sea level

temperature_c.quote: Temperature range from 18 – 32 oC is suitable for better performance

duration_days.quote: First harvest occurs in 70-75 days for recommended varieties; local varieties may require up to 90-100 days

spacing_cm.quote: 75 cm x 60 cm between rows and plants

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: SLS-44: 15-20 t/ha; Krishna: 27 t/ha

description.en: Commonly cultivated in Sri Lanka's Dry Zone, particularly along roadsides, field edges and occasionally in orchards. Large thorny tree reaches 9 m with pinnate citrus-scented leaves. Hard-shelled round pale-brown fruit cracks open to reveal sticky brown pulp with small white seeds — used for *divul kiri* (a yogurt-like refreshment), curries, juices, and chutneys. Two size classes in markets: larger fruits (sweeter, more valuable) and smaller fruits (acidic, less preferred). Tree prefers dry climates for flowering and fruit set.

description.quote: Wood apples, or Divul (Limonia acidissima of the family Rutaceae), are commonly cultivated in the country's dry zones of Sri Lanka. Limonia acidissima is a large tree growing to 9 metres (30 ft) tall with rough, spiny bark. The round, pale brown fruit has a distinctive taste and a musky aroma. It has a hard, woody outer appearance; the fruit is cracked open to reveal a sticky brown pulp with tiny white seeds inside.

spacing_cm.quote: Vine varieties use 30 cm between plants and 90 cm between rows

expected_yield_kg_per_acre.quote: Gannoruwa Hawari produces 35 mt/ha, Gannoruwa A9 yields 21/24 mt/ha, while Polon and Hawari Mae produce 12 mt/ha
